sentences.text: My name is Senta Jerabek, born Beran.
I was born on Prague, on the 10th of May, 1923.
And I'm 72 years old now.
My parents both came from Vienna.
They came after the first world war when my father was offered a very good job in Prague, with a big firm, like, Shell here.
And he was in charge of the whole Sudetenland.
We lived in Prague.
And he went every Monday with the company car and company chauffeur to the Sudetenland and came back again on Friday.
My-- my father was called Tony, and my mother, Lola.
My father was Jewish, and my mother was Catholic.
But obviously, they were not religious, otherwise they wouldn't have married.
Well, I was always aware that I am 1/2 Jewish.
Nobody made any-- any secret out of it.
But because at that time, there were such bad laws, that you couldn't get a divorce either as a Catholic or as a Jew, my father thought I should be Evangelic, which is like Church of England.
And so this is-- we all had-- we had three religions-- three people-- all-- each one had a different religion.
No.
They didn't want anybody after me.
That was enough.
We lived terribly affluent.
It's quite ridiculous today.
We had a maid, and a cook, and a nanny.
And we had a-- a-- a very elaborate apartment.
My mother came from a very rich family.
My father was a self-made man.
And it was terribly important in this time to have Persian carpets and-- and to show off with them.
So as a-- as a child, I had a-- a very lonely life.
I never had other children to play with.
But we were-- you know, having a-- a very good life.
And for holidays, we always went away.
And then I had some children as company.
It was what?
No, I-- I-- I would-- no.
My father definitely wasn't cultured.
He was-- came from a terribly poor family with five children, where the husband ran away and left the woman with the five children.
And my mother was a bit of a snob.
But I don't think what you mean cultured-- I mean, they-- you know, they know how to eat, and how to behave.
But today, we think like, culture is a little bit something else.
I-- I-- oh, they thought we were terribly rich.
And I think it was all-- all like a bubble, you know?
I think that the money-- my father obviously made very good money.
But I think whatever he made went out again.
So except for-- for the-- the-- the things in-- in the-- in the unit, or in the apartment, I don't think they ever had any savings at all.
But at that time, you know, I didn't bother me.
I-- I never thought about it.
Was your father a practicing Jew?
No!
Not at all, mama.
He wouldn't have never married a Catholic.
He went once a year to the temple.
And my mother went once a year, at Easter, to the church-- and then left it up to me if I wanted-- what-- what-- if I want to be religious.
I mean, they did believe in God.
And-- and in the evening, I always had to say a prayer for mommy and daddy.
And-- and that-- that was all the religion I knew.
I went to a-- a private school, [LAUGHS] a German girls school in Prague.
None of us spoke Czech, which is totally ridiculous to live in a country and not speak the language.
And there were mostly Jewish children, because it was an expensive school.
And that is the first time I had friends.
And I-- I picked the ones in the school that were the worst students, like myself.
And I hated school, and I failed right through.
And I left school at 14, and I really had hardly any education.
But I did it, because daddy was the underdog, and mother-- mother was the educated one.
So I wanted to be siding with daddy.
And I thought that will please-- [LAUGHS] please him.
But it didn't please him.
He wanted me to be educated, but I was hopeless.
Well, in that school was-- I think there was both religion-- Jewish and-- and Catholic.
And my parents said I should give it a go and try the Catholic.
And I went to one class, and I came home.
And I said I don't want to do it.
And they left me alone.
I never look-- at that time, nobody thought, are you Jewish?
Are you-- because it-- it didn't matter.
In Prague, it didn't matter.
There was very little anti-Semitism.
At least I think there was a little.
And as a child, you never think about such things at all.
None-- none whatsoever.
And then, of course, the people we mixed wouldn't have been anti-Semites anyhow.
Oh, well, we were aware of it.
Because we went-- when we were in the Sudetenland for-- for vacation, there was a family.
They came from Berlin.
And they-- I must have been maybe 15 or 14.
And they told everybody what is going on in Berlin.
Nobody believed it.
Well, I-- I don't know.
I only know that they had to escape.
And they-- they moved to Czechoslovakia, because they were frightened for their lives.
Oh, there was nothing doing in Czechoslovakia at that time.
I mean, until-- even-- even after the Germans occupied Czechoslovakia, it took a long time before they all organized it, you know, about the Jews.
It took quite a while.
And nobody believed it will last anyhow.
Yes, I-- I can't say now, because I know about it, so I can't say if I knew as a child.
And I think I-- I-- it wouldn't have affected me.
I wouldn't have understood it, because, you know, it's just so unreal, those things.
Unless you-- you lived through them, you-- you wouldn't even understand what is going on.
Well, [LAUGHS] I remember that because of my father; never having spoken properly Czech, and hardly, hardly Czech at all, he lost his job.
And because he spoke only German, the firm could not relocate him in-- in the Czech part that was left over.
So he was out of a job.
And I think that we had no savings.
So I must have been, at the time, about 16.
And my mother straight away started working, selling some display advertising.
And my first job, which I really hated, was selling fashion drawings to dressmakers.
And because my English-- my Czech was so poor, it was such torture, that I just gave up after a short time.
But we had to make money, because all we had were, you know, the carpets and my mother's jewelry.
Well, what happened was that obviously, my-- my parents spoke about it.
And because as-- as soon as the-- the Germans got it organized, they had those rulings that the Jews have to give their fur coats, their jewelery, and their-- their Persian carpets.

And because that was all we really-- never fur coats-- but the other things my mother had-- the only thing was to save, to live from, was for my parents to-- sort of-- just on the outside, to have a divorce.
But it was just to have an official divorce, not a real divorce.
Which meant that my father lived in a next street where he had to sleep, because there was a curfew at 8 o'clock.
And he came, during-- in the morning, he came back into-- into our place.
And-- and in the evening, at 8:00, he had to go again back to-- to where he, you know, where he was officially living.
So that-- that-- the only thing that changed at that time.
Nobody knew that because he is divorced from his Aryan wife, that it will cost his life.
We didn't know that today.
We can all look back and say, why did we do this?
Why did we do that?
So if he would have stayed in the marriage, he would have been protected right through the war.
He wouldn't have been sent away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So did your life changed dramatically after that?
Well, our life changed dramatically only because I had a Jewish boyfriend, and I got very much involved into-- into everything Jewish.
And whatever hurt him, hurt me, or what hurt my father reflected on me.
I was, you know, I-- perfectly all right.
I never had to wear a star.
I-- I wasn't threatened.
I would not have been deported.
And-- and I was doing all right.
And on top of it, I had this marvelous job, eventually, in a German bank-- which was a-- a first real Nazi Bank-- as a telephonist, and make photocopies of all their documents.
And I don't know.
When I filled out a form, obviously, they only ask what religion I have.
They didn't say, is your father a Jew?
So I held the job for over one year.
Tell me, do you remember your father having to wear a star?
Oh, everybody had to wear a star.
I mean when-- when-- when-- when my boyfriend and I went out, I had to either put one on or he had to have a briefcase that he held.
You know, a-- a lot of Jews did that, you know-- have the star when they needed to have it.
And when they didn't need it, they had a briefcase, and they were hiding it behind it.
But that was already very uncomfortable.
I didn't mind putting it on.
March '39, that was obviously when-- when the Germans came.
It wasn't so scary, because we just-- we just had no idea what will eventually happen.
I think that my parents were very distressed when the Germans marched in.
But because I hadn't met my boyfriend in March-- I met him in June-- it-- I mean, sure, I knew it something bad happening.
But look.
When You're 16 years old, those things, they-- they-- they don't seem as real as they really are.
What I saw is-- I think it was they came very early in a-- in the morning.
It-- it was sort of still dark.

And there must have been some news reels, because I wasn't in that-- I mean, I lived in the city, but not where-- where the tanks and everything came, so-- but I must have seen it in the news reel, because I remember when all the tanks and-- and the armies and everything was marching down [NON-ENGLISH], which is [NON-ENGLISH] place.
And-- and there were a lot of people cheering.
You wouldn't believe it.
And-- and there's a lot of people with-- with-- with swastikas with little flags.
So there were quite a few people who thought that it would be lovely.
And they must have been, obviously, German people, because the Czechs certainly never liked the Germans.
Oh, I-- I don't like the Czech people very much.
They-- they-- they always bow to everybody.
They're very servile.
And as soon as-- as the-- the Nazis were there, they were, you know-- I don't want to say [LAUGHS] what I wanted to say.
They were bowing to them, and-- and like they did later on to the communist.
They-- they go with anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, we couldn't go out together unless-- unless he covered his star, or-- or-- we-- my mother-- I could not remember that my mother ever put a star on.
And I don't think that you sort of went walking in the street.
You only went out, I mean, when you did shopping, or-- or for necessary things.
And I might have walked with him to where-- where he was living.
But I can't ever remember, even before this started, the three of us were, you know, going for a walk or something.
We did that when I was a little child, but not later on.
Nobody knew I'm a Jew.
I mean, my hair was very light blonde, and-- and I have blue eyes.
And, I mean, people thought, you know, a Jew is somebody with big nose and black hair.
And my father didn't look Jewish.
My father was, I think, deported on the 19th-- 10-- 9-- no, that was the date-- I-- I do not know.
He must have gone in-- in '41 to Terezin.
And he was deported from-- from Terezin to Treblinka on the 19th-- 10th-- 1942, and murdered.
So that-- this is the last.
And I think that most of those dates that they have in the archives are not very correct.
My mother is working.
Yeah.
My father was distraught when he left his job.
He loved his job, because-- also, that he wasn't all the time at home.
He had sort of a-- a much more interesting life, traveling and coming back home again.
And he aged when he lost his job, even, you know, before the Germans came, before all that happened.
He-- he became, all at once, a very old man.
My job in the bank?
Oh!
It was real fun.
When I applied for the job, I-- at that time, it was very fashionable to have cocoa-colored nail polish.
And I went to the bank manager who had that ad in the-- in the paper.
And-- and he was a Viennese-- a very charming man.
And he was so amused by that 16-year-old with the cocoa-colored fingernails, that he gave me the job.
And he said, but you know, when you come, you better take the nail polish off.
I don't think it-- it would come over very well.
And I was in the first floor.
And downstairs was a normal bank.
And it was a bank for all the Nazis.
For all the people in uniform, that was their bank.
And upstairs, I was, you know, directing the phone calls and doing all the documents that they had.
I did the photocopies.
So, I mean, if I would have been interested, I would have known everything that is going on.
But I wasn't interested.
I mean, I just made the photocopies.
And I was there, I think, nearly a year.
And at that time, already, I-- I have-- had known my boyfriend.
And I think I finished 4 o'clock.
And he always came to pick me up from the bank.
And one day, the bank manager called me.
He knew already by that time that I'm 1/2 Jewish, because I became very friendly with his secretary, who was his lover.
And she wanted some good photos.
And-- and Goldie, my boyfriend, who is called Hans Selig, said OK.
I will take her photos, but tell her-- tell her I'm Jewish.
So I said to her, if you-- if you want the photos, you know, this is how it is.
He is Jewish.
I'm 1/2 Jewish.
And she said, I'm Viennese, I couldn't care less.
I hate the-- I hate the Nazis.
And she must have told the bank manager, her boyfriend.
He knew my background.
But as soon-- as long everything was all right, he didn't mind me having there.
But I got more and more forms to fill out.
And I think-- I can't remember, but something-- there must have been that I couldn't avoid, you know, putting in the truth, and so I-- I got fired.
But that didn't matter very much, because a Czech collaborator, a solicitor, who worked with the bank, knew me from ringing up.
And he spoke to me in Czech.
I-- I spoke a little bit Czech.
And he-- he sort of was chummy with me over the phone.
And when he heard I was being sacked, he gave me a job as his secretary.
I couldn't type.
I couldn't do shorthand.
But I worked for quite a long time and did his correspondence.
I learned how to write in short-- not shorthand, but my own abbreviations and dictation.
And he couldn't speak properly [LAUGHS] German.
I couldn't speak Czech.
But-- but it worked out all right.
So-- so, I mean, I was working right up, I think, till one week till I went to the camp.
Well, I met my boyfriend.
His name is Hans Selig.
But because we were all into Hollywood things and-- and Ginger Rogers and Fred Astaire.
Hans definitely did not agree to me, so I Christianed him Goldie.
And he was a blind date.
And we met in-- in June, 1939.
I was just more than-- a little bit over 16, and he was just a little bit over 17.
And we just fell in love.
And we had-- we, you know, we had-- we had a wonderful time.
We didn't let all-- all the misery disturb our-- that was the first love for both for us.
So it-- it-- it-- it-- it was very, very important to us.
He had just come back in 1939 from a holiday in England.

And his parents, who were millionaires, who had a private bank, who had blocks of land all over Prague, let that boy come back in 1939 after we were already occupied by the Germans, instead of writing to him-- stay in England.
So it-- it was-- I was happy I met him.
But I knew that-- that something is awfully wrong.
He should have never come back.
What-- you had feelings that things were going to be difficult for the Jews?
Well, I think that at that time, you-- you lived in a sort of subconscious fear.
Because you never knew what the next day will bring.
I remember the first winter I was with Goldie.
They had the Jews clean the-- the footpath, the streets, when the snow fell.
And I thought it was very sad, you know, that people who-- who never did any manual work had to go and clean the street.
And Goldie was fantastic.
You know, in the evening he came home.
He said, oh, it was great.
It was such good exercise.
He-- he was such a good sport, such a-- a great guy.
He-- he never-- you know-- he-- a lot of people were complaining.
And-- and he never complained about anything.
But of course, he couldn't go to school.
He couldn't do anything.
I mean, all-- all those Jewish children or teenagers, they-- they had no education at all, because they were not permitted to-- to go to school.
Well, his father was very old.
He was-- father was born in 19-- 1872.
So he was over 60 when I came into that family.
He was obviously a very, very wealthy man.
He was religious.
And Goldie absolutely hated all-- all those religious things with those, you know, things that you put on your head and around your arm.
And the old men still did that.
And-- and Goldie made fun of him.
And the wife was a ex-secretary.
And she was 25 years younger.
So that family was sort of-- so divided within.
But to me, the mother and the two sons were terrific.
And-- and they adopted me.
I-- I thought I belonged-- never belonged so much in my own home, than I belonged with them.
And-- and I-- I-- you know-- and they were living nearly five minutes walk from me.
So I was, most of the time, in their place.
Because the Jews couldn't go out after 8:00.
There was, eventually, a curfew.
And I could go out.
So I-- I was spending a lot of time with them.
And-- and I-- I loved the mother.
And-- and I liked his brother very much.
Well, his aspirations were-- he-- he-- I think he was playing very well tenor saxophone.
And he was a very good photographer.
But I think his aspirations were electronics.
He was always fiddling around.
And I remember-- I don't know how it was.
It sounds quite crazy now.
It was a piece of salt.
And he put some wiring in it and played like a radio.
[LAUGHS] I don't know how he did it and what he did.
But he-- he was very bright.
And-- and that-- that is what, I think, under normal circumstances, he would have done.
Oh, I was a typical teenager.
I mean, I-- all I wanted was to go to Hollywood and be a movie star.
And so-- I-- I mean, with my education, what could I have done?
I mean, I'm practical.
I can pick up a lot of things.
But I mean, in normal times, I had no qualifications whatsoever.
But I never needed them.
I always got so--
Did I have plans?
Together.
Well, I-- I don't know.
We, of course, yes.
Like every young people in love, we were talking that one day we will get married.
And he said, but-- but it would be good if we have other relationships eventually.
Because just the first love-- it's very dangerous to settle down.
I didn't like that at all.
I was terribly possessive.
And-- and then we started talking about children.
And I actually never really wanted to have children.
But because I was so much in love with him, I went with everything along.
And so we talked how they will look.
And I said, oh.
I love if they look like you.
And he said, they should look like-- like you.
And so-- so we talked like probably all-- all teenagers talk when they're in love.
Well, he was the first transport that went to Theresienstadt, which is Terezin, in Czech.
And there-- there was a group of 1,000 young men.
And they went into that Theresienstadt, which is-- which is a garrison town.
And in the army barracks, they had to prepare for the influx, which at that time, also we didn't know.
So they had to build for more and more people, obviously, those beds-- you know, the three-floor beds, and all that.
And the people were so rich, they could have bought the whole Jewish council.
Why they didn't try to get those boys out of the transport-- and still they were ways to get them out of Czechoslovakia-- I never understood.
I even had this crazy idea to say maybe if Goldie would marry me, because I'm 1/2 Jewish, I-- I could protect him.
Nobody ever listened to me.
Anyhow--
Oh, at that time we knew-- knew it-- it is starting.
Something bad is starting.
I mean, there were always little bits and bits of something happening, like that the Jews have to hand over.
They took all the properties away.
Of course, they took that private bank away from the family.
They still had the block of flat where-- where they had the apartment.
So we knew that it's-- it's getting worse than better.
So when-- when Goldie got-- it-- it was-- his number was 507.
And he and his brother were both in that transport.
I-- I just-- I-- it-- I only had a year and a 1/2 with him.
I-- I was heartbroken that he went away.
And when he went away, it was in winter.
It was in December.
And I just couldn't bear to be in-- in a warm bed and to have a good meal, because I-- I was just frantic thinking that he will be cold.
He will be hungry.
And eventually, there must have been more women or girls in my position.
And-- and some, obviously, were much smarter than I, and older.
And they started to go into to ghetto.
The ghetto was still not divided.
There were still Czech families living there.
And the Jews were only in the army barracks.
And the women got into the ghettos to see their boyfriend.
And so eventually, I found out that Goldie had arranged for me to come and where to meet him.
At that time, the train didn't go into Theresienstadt.
It went to a little township by Auschwitz.
I don't know how long it was.
You had to walk on-- on the road about 1/2 an hour, 3/4 an hour.
So I-- I was-- I was frightened, but I was also very excited.
And-- and I went to his mother.
And I said, look.
Get as much food on the black market I can-- that I can carry.
And I'm-- I'm meeting Goldie.
And she says, oh, don't go.
Don't go.
And I said, oh, I will go.
I was so scared.
But because I said it already to him, I knew that I can't back out.
So I went.
And I-- I went to the-- my train, to that station.
And then I went and I wore a swastika.
And I went to that German hotel where some German people were living.
I think the soldiers were meeting, again, with their girlfriends when they were on leave, and the girlfriends came there visiting them.
So I took my room.
And at night, I can't remember if it was midnight, or-- I know it had to be very dark.
And it was a certain hour.
And I knew where I should go.
I had a look before, you know, while it was still light.
And it was called Bauhof.
It was a big shed where they were building things.
And I went to that gate.
It-- it was a big gate.
And-- and I knocked, and the door opened.
And behind was Goldie.
And his hair was shorn off.
And-- and I was so upset.
And-- and we just hugged.
And we were both so frightened.
And I gave him the-- the two bags with the food.
And he gave me a lot of messages to take to Prague, again, from Jewish boys, for non-Jewish girlfriends.
And I did that trip nine times.
But in the middle, I think the fourth-- the fourth trip or the fifth, he gave me a film.
